title: Where did the 43 come from — and is it still fresh?

steps.narration: 14:20. A risk analyst pings the on-call: "The exec dashboard says 43 orders in the fraud-review queue. Where does that number actually come from? I need to defend it in the compliance review at 15:00." There is no good answer from memory — only from lineage. We walk the number backward, one hop at a time.

state.note: the question every data engineer eventually gets paged for: where did this number come from? — answered by lineage, not by guessing

steps.narration: Hop 1 — the Gold layer. The dashboard reads one cell of gold.fraud_review_queue, a mart with exactly 43 rows. Its catalog entry names one upstream input: silver.orders_scored, and one transform: WHERE fraud_score >= FRAUD_REVIEW_THRESHOLD. The 0.80 rule is not folklore; it is a recorded edge in the lineage graph.

state.note: Gold ← silver.orders_scored via filter(fraud_score >= FRAUD_REVIEW_THRESHOLD) — one node, one edge, one predicate

steps.narration: Hop 2 — the Silver layer. silver.orders_scored holds 225 rows: every order that carries a fraud score. It is an INNER JOIN of two Bronze tables on order_id. The filter drops it 225 → 43; the join is where the row count was already set. To explain 43 we first have to explain 225.

Hop 3 — the Bronze sources, the leaves of the graph. bronze.orders has 240 rows; bronze.fraud_scores has 225. The inner join keeps only matches, so the 15 unscored orders (240 − 225) never reach Silver. That gap is not a bug — it is a documented lineage fact, and it is exactly why the queue can never exceed 225.

state.note: leaves of the lineage graph: two raw tables from the ingestion layer — nothing upstream of them

steps.narration: The full chain, read bottom-up: orders (240) + fraud_scores (225) → inner join → orders_scored (225) → filter >= FRAUD_REVIEW_THRESHOLD → fraud_review_queue (43). Column-level lineage adds the detail that matters in the compliance room: 4 of the 43 sit exactly at 0.80, kept only because the predicate is inclusive (>=, not >). Swap that operator and the number becomes 39.

state.note: answer to the 14:20 page: 240 → ⋈ → 225 → filter(>= FRAUD_REVIEW_THRESHOLD) → 43, and 4 rows ride the inclusive boundary

steps.narration: Lineage answered where. Observability has a second question the analyst hasn't asked yet: is the number still true right now? A correct value computed over stale inputs is still wrong. Every table in the catalog carries a freshness SLO — a promise about how old its data may be before a consumer should stop trusting it.

state.note: freshness SLO: gold.delivery_sla must be built from courier data no more than 60 min behind event time

steps.narration: 17:05. The delivery-SLA tile on the same dashboard is fed by bronze.courier_pings — event-time data, where ingested_at lags event_ts. Most pings land within a few minutes. But tonight a batch of couriers went through a dead-zone, and their pings arrive with ingested_at up to 95–130 minutes after the event actually happened.

state.note: late-arriving data: ingested_at − event_ts blows past the 60-min freshness budget for a slice of courier_pings

steps.narration: The freshness monitor computes lag as now − max(event_ts seen) for the partition feeding gold.delivery_sla. It crosses 60 minutes at 17:05 and keeps climbing. The mart itself looks healthy — rows present, job green — but its newest event is now over an hour old. The dashboard is confidently showing yesterday's delivery picture.

state.note: SLO breach: 95 min > 60 min — the metric is stale even though every job succeeded

steps.narration: The alert fires — and because the catalog carries lineage, it fires with a blast radius, not just a red dot. It names every downstream node that inherits the staleness: gold.delivery_sla, the two dashboard tiles built on it, and the daily ops digest email. Crucially it also names what is not affected: fraud_review_queue has no courier_pings ancestor, so the 43 is stale-proof here.

state.note: alert = freshness_breach(gold.delivery_sla, lag=95min, slo=60min) → downstream: 2 dashboard tiles + ops digest · fraud_review_queue unaffected (no shared ancestor)

steps.narration: Triage is now mechanical instead of archaeological. Lineage points straight at the leaf — courier_pings, the dead-zone batch — so nobody re-derives the pipeline by reading code at 17:30. The fix is a watermark: hold delivery_sla from publishing until late data settles, or mark the tile STALE until lag drops back under the 60-min SLO. Either way the dashboard stops lying.

state.note: resolution: watermark + STALE badge gated on the freshness SLO — recover to 12 min, back inside budget

steps.narration: Two questions, two disciplines, one catalog. Lineage answered where did 43 come from — a graph you traverse, not a memory you trust: 240 → 225 → 43, with 4 rows on the 0.80 boundary. Freshness SLOs answered is it still true — a clock on every table, with an alert that already knows its own blast radius. A number you can neither trace nor date is a number you cannot defend.

state.note: the observable pipeline: every metric has a lineage graph (where it came from) and a freshness clock (how old it is) — both recorded, both queryable, neither guessed
